coredata.dc:title: A promising tool for clinical diagnostics: Dual-emissive carbonized polymer dots based cross-linking enhanced emission for sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase and butyrylcholinesterase
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tingting
Wang, Dan
Hu, Jing
Fu, Xu
Ji, Yibing
Li, Ruijun
coredata.dc:description: Compared with single signal readout, dual-signal readout commendably corrects the impact of systematic or background error, achieving more accurate results for the diagnosis of many diseases. This work aimed to design and prepare dual-emissive fluorescent probes for the construction of ratiometric fluorescence biosensors to detect liver disease biomarkers. Sodium alginate (SA) with numerous potential sub-fluorophores and active sites and 4,4′,4′′,4′′′-(porphine-5,10,15,20-tetrayl) tetrakis (benzoic acid) (TCPP) with macrocyclic conjugated structures were introduced to prepare the carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with red/blue dual emission based on the cross-linking enhanced emission (CEE) effect and the luminescence of macrocyclic conjugated structures. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems were constructed by integrating the specific response of CPDs to Cu2+ and the affinity difference of Cu2+ to substrates or products of enzymes. The sensing systems, CPDs/Cu2+/PPi and CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh, were designed to detect liver disease biomarkers, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), respectively. The limit of detection for ALP and BChE was 0.35 U/L and 0.19 U/L, respectively. The proposed sensors were successfully applied to human serum samples from different health stages with satisfactory recoveries. These results demonstrate the successful design of a novel dual-emissive fluorescent probe and provide a feasible strategy for clinical detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emissive
Cross-linking enhanced emission
Carbonized polymer dots
Alkaline phosphatase
Butyrylcholinesterase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the dual-emissive carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)
Activity detection of enzymes by dual-emissive CPDs
Activity detection of enzymes in human serum
Working principle of the dual-emissive CPDs ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Condition optimization of dual-emissive CPDs for detecting ALP and BChE
Analytical performance of dual-emissive CPDs for detecting ALP and BChE
Quantification of enzymes activity in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 21804141 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CPDs; (b) AFM image of CPDs, inset: the height profile along the line in (b); (c)TGA curve of CPDs, (d) FT-IR of CPDs, TCPP and SA (e) XPS spectrum of CPDs, (f) Emission and excitation spectra of CPDs.
(a) Preparation of dual-emissive CPDs; (b) Application of CPDs for constructing ratiometric fluorescence sensors for ALP and BChE assay.

(a) UV/vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+, emission and excitation spectra of CPDs. (b) UV/vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+, CPDs, and CPDs + Cu2+. Fluorescence decay lifetime of CPDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ (c) λem = 450 nm and (d) λem = 650 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs/Cu2+/PPi sensing system responding to ALP activities (0.5–200 U/L), (b) The corresponding fitting curves for I450/I650 and ALP activities (0.5–200 U/L), (c) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh sensing system responding to BChE activities (0.5–100 U/L), (d) The plot fitting of I650/I450 as a function of the BChE activities (0.5–35 U/L).
Effect of metal ions, amino acids, enzymes and other interferences on (a) ALP assay and (b) BChE assay.
Determination of ALP and BChE activities in human serum samples with both normal and abnormal liver function (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A promising tool for clinical diagnostics: Dual-emissive carbonized polymer dots based cross-linking enhanced emission for sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase and butyrylcholinesterase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 210009, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance, Ministry of Education, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Emergency Medicine, Lanzhou University Second Hospital, Gansu, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, 24 Tong Jia Xiang, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210009, China.
Corresponding author. Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, 24 Tong Jia Xiang, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210009, China.
Corresponding author. Laboratory of Emergency Medicine, Lanzhou University Second Hospital, Gansu, Lanzhou, 730000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared with single signal readout, dual-signal readout commendably corrects the impact of systematic or background error, achieving more accurate results for the diagnosis of many diseases. This work aimed to design and prepare dual-emissive fluorescent probes for the construction of ratiometric fluorescence biosensors to detect liver disease biomarkers. Sodium alginate (SA) with numerous potential sub-fluorophores and active sites and 4,4′,4′′,4′′′-(porphine-5,10,15,20-tetrayl) tetrakis (benzoic acid) (TCPP) with macrocyclic conjugated structures were introduced to prepare the carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with red/blue dual emission based on the cross-linking enhanced emission (CEE) effect and the luminescence of macrocyclic conjugated structures. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems were constructed by integrating the specific response of CPDs to Cu2+ and the affinity difference of Cu2+ to substrates or products of enzymes. The sensing systems, CPDs/Cu2+/PPi and CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh, were designed to detect liver disease biomarkers, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), respectively. The limit of detection for ALP and BChE was 0.35 U/L and 0.19 U/L, respectively. The proposed sensors were successfully applied to human serum samples from different health stages with satisfactory recoveries. These results demonstrate the successful design of a novel dual-emissive fluorescent probe and provide a feasible strategy for clinical detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent biosensors have great potential for analytical sensing and optical imaging(Li et al., 2023; Song et al., 2023). However, the accuracy of single-fluorescent biosensors is usually affected by environmental fluctuations, light scattering from the sample, instrumental efficiency, and local concentration variation of the optical probe(Gui et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, ratiometric fluorescent biosensors with self-calibration have gained attention as a strategy for these issues(Chu et al., 2017; Fu et al., 2022). Many researchers have hybridized two probes with different emission wavelengths to develop ratiometric fluorescent sensors (Haddad Irani-nezhad et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). Cheng et al. (2019) established a ratiometric fluorescent platform containing both blue-emitting carbon dots and yellow-emitting carbon dots for determining the activity of enzymes. Gao et al. (2021)successfully fabricated a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting vitamin B12 based on dual-emitting carbonized polymer dots to simplify the procedures of combining two different emissive probes. Regrettably, the two fluorescent emissive signals were obtained at different excitation wavelengths, making it impossible to obtain a fluorescence spectrum simultaneously. Although there have been a few reports on dual-emissive fluorescent probes at the same excitation wavelength, a clear and simple strategy for design remains a challenge(Saboorizadeh and Zare-Dorabei, 2022; Xia et al., 2022).

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are a type of carbon dots (CDs) composed of short polymer chains with numerous nitrogen/oxygen-based groups and an inner carbonized core made of sp2-hybridized carbon structures within sp3-hybridized frameworks(Zeng et al., 2021). Therefore, the CPDs possess not only the excellent properties of CDs but also a special luminescence mechanism called cross-linking enhanced emission (CEE) that is characteristic of polymers(Tao et al., 2020). The CEE proposed that the fluorescence of sub-fluorophores (CO, CN, NO, or amino-based groups, C–O) could be enhanced by covalent bond cross-linking, supramolecular contact, or aggregation within cross-linked structures(Tao et al., 2019). Hence, the CEE effect can guide the selection of an appropriate precursor to synthesize dual-emissive CPDs and construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for biomarker analysis.

Enzymes are widespread in living organisms and play a crucial role in regulating metabolic processes. Abnormal enzyme activity has been associated with certain diseases(Apatzidou et al., 2018; Franjesevic et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2021). Clinically, butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in serum are essential indicators for diagnosing liver diseases like liver dysfunction, liver cirrhosis, and chronic hepatitis(Crotty et al., 2020; Price and Sammons, 1974). Abnormal expressions of ALP and BChE have also been associated with hyperthyroidism, diabetes, high blood pressure, and cancer(Haarhaus et al., 2019; Sicinska et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2019). Thus, developing an efficient, convenient, and highly sensitive method for detecting ALP and BChE activity in serum is important for the early diagnosis and prognostication of associated diseases. Hitherto, fluorometric, colorimetric, electrochemical, and Raman spectroscopy assays were used to detect ALP and BChE activity (Cao et al., 2022; Nechaeva et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2016). Among these methods, the ratiometric fluorescence method has obtained great attention owing to its high sensitivity, accuracy, and low cost. However, most ratiometric fluorescent probes emit blue or green light, which limits their applications due to photodamage caused by UV excitation light and autofluorescence interference from the sample matrix(Han et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2021). In contrast, red-emissive fluorescent probes with longer emissive wavelengths can reduce the effect of autofluorescence from the sample matrix(Su et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022). Therefore, designing a dual-emissive probe with red emission for a ratiometric fluorescence biosensor has become a challenge(Lesani et al., 2021).

In this study, based on the mechanism of cross-linking enhanced emission (CEE), dual-emissive CPDs have been first designed. We introduced sodium alginate (SA) with numerous potential sub-fluorophores and active sites and 4,4′,4′′,4′′′-(porphine-5,10,15,20-tetrayl) tetrakis (benzoic acid) (TCPP) with macrocyclic conjugated structures to prepare dual-emissive CPDs. When excited at 385 nm, the obtained CPDs present a special dual-emission pattern, with blue emission at 450 nm and red emission at 650 nm. Due to the strong quenching impact of Cu2+ on the red emission and the affinity difference of Cu2+ to the substrates or products of enzymes, the CPDs were employed for developing ratiometric fluorescence sensors to detect ALP and BChE in linear detection ranges of 0.5–200 U/L and 0.5–35 U/L with a low limit of detection (LOD) of approximately 0.35 U/L and 0.19 U/L, respectively. Compared to conventional detection approaches with ALP and BChE, the CPDs used the CEE and macrocyclic conjugated structures to achieve dual emission, and the proposed sensors exhibited good analytical performance in human serum with both normal and abnormal liver function. Therefore, this method has the potential to serve as a new method for clinical detection.
A detailed description of all materials and apparatus was provided in Section S1 in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the dual-emissive carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CPDs were prepared through the solvothermal method. SA (30 mg) and TCPP (10 mg) were added to 10 mL of DMF. The mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer and ultrasonicated until a homogeneous solution was obtained. Then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-line autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h, resulting in a reddish-brown solution. The solution was filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed (MWCO: 1000 Da) against deionized water for 24 h. The powder was obtained through freeze-dried and dispersed in the deionized water and stored at 4 °C. Details were provided in Section S2 in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity detection of enzymes by dual-emissive CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: For ALP, a standard enzyme solution of ALP at different activity level were added into Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 7.4) that contained CPDs (60 μL), the substrate PPi (100 μM), and Cu2+ (15 μM). The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation at 385 nm.

For BChE, a standard enzyme solution of BChE at different activity level were added into Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 6.0) containing the substrate BTCh (150 μM), and Cu2+ (8 μM). The mixture was incubated for 50 min. Then, 60 μL of CPDs were added into the above solution and incubated for 30 min. All incubations were incubated at 37 °C. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation at 385 nm.
The total volume of the mixture was 600 μL for the activity detection of enzymes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity detection of enzymes in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum samples were obtained from Lanzhou University Second Hospital including both normal and abnormal liver function, and were diluted with deionized water before detection. The samples were diluted 5 fold for the ALP assay and 50 fold for the BChE assay. Then, 30 μL of each serum sample was added to the above reaction solution containing various activity levels of enzyme solution, and the subsequent procedures were identical to those described in Section 2.3.

The morphology, functional groups, and optical properties of CPDs were characterized by TEM, AFM, FT-IR and XPS. The TEM images (Fig. 1 a) revealed that the morphology of CPDs was spherical, with a size of about 3.94 nm (Fig. S2). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, which differed from that of the graphene quantum dots. It was possible that the process of cross-linking and carbonization influence the formation of lattice spacing(Liu et al., 2018). The average height of the CPDs as measured by AFM was 9.71 nm, which was larger than the size observed by TEM (Fig. 1b and Fig. S3). The deviation could be attributed to the degradation of polymer chains under a strong electrical field and low contrast of polymer structures, resulting in the TEM recording only the morphology of the carbon core of CPDs(Liu et al., 2020a). The TGA analysis of CPDs were displayed in Fig. 1c.TGA curve showed three-stage degradation of CPDs. The first degradation from 20 °C to 120 °C was ascribed to decomposition from the evaporation of bound and free water(Kassaee et al., 2011). The second degradation from 130 °C to 300 °C was attributed to decomposition from the organic functional groups (hydroxyl, amide, unsaturated double bond)(Huang et al., 2017; Xun et al., 2023). The last degradation from 300 °C to 620 °C described the pyrolysis of inner cross-linked polymer chains and polymer networks(Liu et al., 2020b). The final residual weight was 40.0% over 620 °C thermal degradation, indicating weight percentage of carbon cores in CPDs. These results demonstrated that the CPDs were composed of a carbon core and polymer chain shell.

As shown in Fig. 1d, the FT-IR of SA showed absorption peaks at 3475 cm-1 and 1616 cm-1, which were attributed to the O–H stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration peak. The characteristic peak at 1304 cm-1 came from the C–O bond in the glycosidic bond. The FT-IR of TCPP exhibited the peaks of O–H and N–H at around 3313 cm-1, the CO stretching vibration peak belonging to -COOH (1690 cm-1), and the aromatic structure contraction vibration peaks of the porphyrin ring present at 1606 cm-1 and 1400 cm-1(Ling et al., 2015). Furthermore, the FT-IR of CPDs demonstrated a characteristic peak of the C–O bond (1304 cm-1), stretching vibration peaks of O–H and N–H at around 3433 cm-1, and the stretching vibration peak of CO (1690 cm-1), as well as the peaks at 1606 cm-1 and 1390 cm-1, which referred to the aromatic structure contraction vibration peaks of the porphyrin ring. These results indicated that CPDs were synthesized by SA and TCPP and contained -OH and -COOH functional groups. Moreover, the presence of a porphyrin ring structure in CPDs was also confirmed.

The XPS analysis of CPDs were displayed in Fig. 1e, confirming the presence of Na, C, N, and O elements in the CPDs. High-resolution scans were performed on the C, O, and N elements of the CPDs. The observed signals of CO, C–O, and O–Na provided evidence that the CPDs contained carboxylic sodium moieties (Fig. S4). Moreover, the C–N–C signal in Fig. S4 indicated the existence of a porphyrin ring structure in CPDs.

The fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 1f displayed maximum emission at 450 nm and 650 nm. When the excitation wavelengths increased from 340 to 400 nm, a red shift excitation-dependent manner was observed for the blue emission (Fig. S5). The excitation-dependent manner is always observed on CDs because of the different particle sizes and diverse emissive trap sites(Xia et al., 2019). It was highly probable that the blue emission of CPDs originated from the different sizes of polymer structures formed by cross-linking carbonization due to the cross-linking enhanced emission effect (Fig. 2 a). The excitation-independent phenomena occurred on the red emission at 650 nm in Fig. S5. Therefore, the red emission might come from porphyrins with macrocyclic conjugated structures in CPDs (Fig. 2a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the dual-emissive CPDs ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the proposed sensor for ALP/BChE assay was based on the specific response of CPDs to Cu2+ and the affinity difference for Cu2+ to substrate/product of ALP/BChE. The CPDs/Cu2+/PPi sensing platform was employed to detect ALP, where the coordination of PPi and Cu2+ could block the quenching process between Cu2+ and CPDs. Upon the addition of ALP, PPi was dephosphorylated to release Cu2+, and the fluorescence of CPDs at 650 nm was quenched by free Cu2+. The CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh sensing platform was used for the BChE assay. BTCh had little affinity for Cu2+ and thus cannot prevent the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+ on CPDs. However, TCh, the product of BChE catalyzed BTCh, with a high affinity for Cu2+, so that TCh could be coordinated with Cu2+ to restore the fluorescence of CPDs.

The quenching effect of Cu2+ on CPDs was essential for the proposed sensor. We further investigated the mechanism of quenching. The UV–visible spectrum of Cu2+ hardly overlapped with the fluorescence emission or excitation spectrum of CPDs (Fig. 3 a) which ruled out the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism and the inner filter effect. The UV–visible spectrum of CPDs displayed characteristic absorption peaks of the porphyrin ring, including a major Soret peak at 412 nm and four Q-band peaks at 516 nm, 552 nm, 580 nm, and 640 nm (Fig. 3b)(Cao et al., 2021). The Soret major peak at 412 nm remained in the UV–visible spectrum of CPDs + Cu2+, while the other four absorption peaks disappeared and a new absorption peak appeared at 540 nm. We postulated that Cu2+ altered the electron distribution surrounding the porphyrin ring, leading to the disappearance of the absorption peaks of the Q-band. The formation of a novel complex between Cu2+ and the porphyrin ring of CPDs was indicated by the appearance of a new absorption peak at 540 nm (Fig. S6). Therefore, Cu2+ had a static quenching effect on CPDs.

In Fig. 3(c-d), the fluorescence lifetimes of CPDs were investigated in the presence and absence of Cu2+. The addition of Cu2+ resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime at 650 nm from 4.57 ns to 0.97 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime at 450 nm remained constant at 0.75 ns, indicating that dynamic quenching occurs between Cu2+ and CPDs. Based on the Stern-Volmer equation, the reduction in fluorescence intensity and lifetime in the presence of the quencher is proportional in dynamic quenching (I0/I = τ0/τ)(Hu et al., 2022), while for static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime was not affected. Under the same conditions, the value of I0/I was greater than τ0/τ, confirming that the quenching mechanism of Cu2+ on CPDs involved both dynamic and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization of dual-emissive CPDs for detecting ALP and BChE
ce:section.ce:para: Several crucial experimental parameters, including pH, incubation time, and the concentration of Cu2+ and substrate (PPi/BTCh) were optimized to obtain the best analytical performance.

The optimizations of the ALP assay were depicted in Fig. S7. The quenching efficiency of Cu2+ increased with pH in the range of 5.0 and 7.4. The maximal quenching efficiency was obtained at pH = 7.4. As the concentration of Cu2+ increased, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I650/I450) gradually declined. This indicated that the fluorescence of CPDs at 650 nm decreased with increasing Cu2+ concentration until it finally disappeared. Hence, 15 μM was chosen as the optimal concentration of Cu2+. Fig. S7c exhibited that I650/I450 increased with increasing PPi concentrations, reaching a maximum of fluorescence recovery degrees of CPDs at PPi concentrations above 100 μM, which was used in the subsequent experiments. Finally, the appropriate incubation time was 60 min, as shown in Fig. S7d.

The related parameters of the BChE assay were also examined due to the use of different substrates in the CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh sensing system. Fig. S8a displayed that the fluorescence recovery efficiency increased with pH and then decreased when pH was above 6.0, indicating that the sensor (CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh) performed optimally at pH = 6.0. For improved analytical performance, the optimal concentrations of Cu2+ and BTCh were determined to be 8 μM and 150 μM, respectively. The procedures for the BChE assay involved two steps: in the first step, BChE catalyzed BTCh to produce TCh, which then coordinated with Cu2+; in the second step, excess Cu2+ quenched the fluorescence of CPDs. Fig. S8 (d-e) illustrated that the optimal incubation times for the two steps were 50 min and 30 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of dual-emissive CPDs for detecting ALP and BChE
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performances (linearity, limit of detection (LOD), and selectivity) of the proposed sensors were evaluated under optimal conditions. With the increase of ALP activities, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs at 650 nm gradually decreased, while that at 450 nm remained unchanged (Fig. 4 a). The ALP activity was proportional to the ratio of I450/I650, with a linear relationship expressed as I450/I650 = 0.0205c + 0.5365 (R2 = 0.9998) in the range of 0.5–200 U/L and a LOD of 0.35 U/L (Fig. 4b). The analytical performance of the sensor was superior to most previously reported works (Table S1).

In contrast to the ALP sensing phenomenon, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs at 650 nm increased as the BChE activities increased (Fig. 4c). A good linear relationship between I650/I450 and BChE activity was observed in the range of 0.5–35 U/L, as represented by the equation I650/I450 = 0.0426c + 0.2128 (R2 = 0.9907). The LOD for the BChE assay was 0.19 U/L. The sensor displayed better analytical performance for the BChE assay compared to previously reported works (Table S2).

Biological interferences, including metal ions, amino acids, enzymes, and other small molecules in serum, were analyzed to evaluate the specificity of the proposed sensing platform. Glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys) with reducing capacities inhibited the quenching efficiency of Cu2+. The interference effects were prevented by the masking effect of N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), which could specifically react with the thiol group of GSH and Cys. As shown in Fig. 5 a, ALP caused a significant increase in the ratio of I450/I650, whereas acid phosphatase (ACP) also induced an increase in the ratio, but significantly less than that caused by ALP. In Fig. 5b, only BChE significantly increased the ratio of I650/I450, whereas other interferences caused minor changes. Therefore, the results proved that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-emissive CPDs had satisfactory selectivity for ALP and BChE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of enzymes activity in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the potential of dual-emissive CPDs in practical applications, ALP and BChE activities were measured in several human serum samples with both normal and abnormal liver function. As shown in Table 1 , the CPDs/Cu2+/PPi sensing system was utilized for ALP assay in serum samples from individuals with normal liver function, the recoveries ranged from 93.1% to 102.2%, with RSD less than 4.5%. For serum samples from individuals with abnormal liver function, the recoveries ranged from 98.1% to 109.5%, with an RSD of less than 3.2%.

The BChE activities were detected by the CPDs/Cu2+/BTCh sensing system in serum samples obtained from individuals with both normal and abnormal liver function. The recoveries for serum samples with normal liver function were approximately 95.6% -101.1% with RSD less than 6.8%. The recoveries for serum samples with abnormal liver function ranged from 99.1% to 103.2% with an RSD less than 4.7%. The above results suggested the feasibility of the ratiometric sensor based on dual-emissive CPDs for detecting ALP and BChE activity in biological samples.

In summary, a novel design strategy for a dual-emissive fluorescence probe with blue/red emission was successfully proposed using the cross-linking enhanced emission principle. A new ratiometric fluorescence sensor that integrated the catalytic reactions of ALP and BChE, along with the specific response of CPDs to Cu2+ and the different affinity between Cu2+ and the substrates/products of enzymes for ALP/BChE assay. The proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensors exhibited good analytical performance in detecting ALP and BChE activity and displayed considerable potential for practical applications. However, the subsequent work should simplify the procedures for assay, preferably by realizing a one-step analysis. At the same time, the proposed method should closely integrate with clinical practice to maximize the value of applications.

Tingting Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Dan Wang: Formal analysis, Verification. Jing Hu: Validation. Xu Fu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yibing Ji: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Ruijun Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 21804141].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2023.115576.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Apatzidou, D.A., Iskas, A., Konstantinidis, A., Alghamdi, A.M., Tumelty, M., Lappin, D.F., Nile, C.J., 2018. J. Clin. Periodontol. 45(10), 1173-1183.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cheng, X., Xu, J., Wang, L., Xu, G., Wei, F., Chai, Y., Hu, Q., Cen, Y., 2019. Mikrochim. Acta 186(12), 818-826.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chu et al., 2017
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chu, B., Song, B., Ji, X., Su, Y., Wang, H., He, Y., 2017. Anal. Chem. 89(22), 12152-12159.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Crotty, R., Taylor, M.S., Farmer, J.R., Kakar, S., Yilmaz, F., Ardeniz O., Patil, D.T., Deshpande, V., 2020. Am. J. Surg. Pathol. 44(5), 617-625.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Franjesevic et al., 2019
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Franjesevic, A.J., Sillart, S.B., Beck, J.M., Vyas, S., Callam, C.S., Hadad, C.M., 2019. Chem-A Eur J 25(21), 5337-5371.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Fu et al., 2022
